audit_method: direct URL fetch — strip-first repair, 2026-06-12. Retained rows were verified by curl/PDF text extraction; no WebSearch snippets used.

rows.supporting_quote: When the Expanding Housing Affordability (EHA) ordinance became effective on July 1, 2022, it updated the affordable housing linkage fee ... and created new affordable housing requirements for developments of 10 or more new dwelling units.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: The mandatory affordable housing program and the linkage fee are intended to complement existing tools and funding resources to better meet Denver’s growing housing needs.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch; PDF converted with pdftotext.

rows.supporting_quote: new residential development of 10 or more units must build on-site affordable housing ... High Market Area 10% of total dwelling units 60% of AMI ... Typical Market Area 8% of total dwelling units 60% of AMI ... IRUs shall be maintained as affordable for a minimum term of 99-years.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: Staff Recommendation for high market areas ... Rental: 10% of total units up to 60% AMI ... Rental: 15% of total units averaging 70% AMI ... typical market areas ... Rental: 8% of total units up to 60% AMI ... Rental: 12% of total units averaging up to 70% AMI.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch; PDF converted with pdftotext.

rows.supporting_quote: The Expanding Housing Affordability ordinance became effective July 1, 2022 and increases linkage fees for non-residential development and residential development of 9 or fewer units.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: Projects that do not meet the above requirements are subject to the 2022 linkage fee based on the City and County of Denver Ordinance 20220426. The affordable housing linkage fee is based on the gross floor area (GFA) of your project as defined by the International Building Code.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch; PDF converted with pdftotext.

rows.supporting_quote: Denver’s affordable housing linkage fee to increase July 1 ...
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch. Used only for July 2025 rate-increase context.

rows.supporting_quote: A federal judge has thrown out a Denver homebuilder’s lawsuit ... U.S. District Judge Philip Brimmer ... March 3 decision ... Because its case was dismissed without prejudice, RedT can file an amended lawsuit.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch; PLF complaint source s5 returned 404 and was dropped.

rows.supporting_quote: September 19: Ordinance approved during second/final reading at Council ... revenue projection of an estimated $150 million and 6,000 units over the first 10 years ... property tax ... fee on development ... approved dedicated property tax of 0.5 mills.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: Hancock signed into law an ordinance raising the local marijuana tax from 3.5 percent to 5.5 percent. The increase is expected to double the number of affordable housing units created by the city’s housing fund over the next five years.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: Denver increasing its local special sales tax on recreational marijuana purchases from 3.5 to 5.5 percent in 2018 to help fund local affordable housing programs ... the 2 percent increase ... provided nearly $38.8 million from late 2018 through 2022.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch. Original through-2023 $45.5M claim dropped.

rows.supporting_quote: The Department of Housing Stability builds a healthy, housed, and connected Denver. We invest resources, create policy, and partner with organizations ...
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: HOST plans to invest $229 million ... including over $61 million for the All In Mile High initiative, $1.2 million for cold weather shelter expansion, and $23 million for rent and utility assistance.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch; PDF converted with pdftotext.

rows.supporting_quote: On July 18, 2023, Denver Mayor Mike Johnston signed an emergency declaration ... move 1,000 people ... Through the House1000 initiative ... That goal was successfully met by the end of 2023 ... launched All In Mile High (AIMH) in 2024.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: 7,003 people have come inside from the streets, and more than 5,597 people have moved into permanent housing since 2023.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch; PDF converted with pdftotext.

rows.supporting_quote: This interim report documents the early implementation of Denver’s All In Mile High (AIMHigh) initiative ... applying a place-based encampment resolution approach ... moving individuals to new noncongregate shelters ... connecting people to permanent housing.
rows.notes: Direct curl fetch.

rows.supporting_quote: Flo ... seventh and final multifamily building ... replacing 333 outdated public housing units with 965 modern, mixed-income homes ... 20%–100% AMI ... $30 million Choice Neighborhoods ... over $60 million ... over $500 million.

rows.supporting_quote: DHA is underway in replacing 333 units of public housing in the development of 940 new homes ...

rows.supporting_quote: Flo is the seventh and final multifamily building ... replacement of 333 outdated public housing units with 965 modern, mixed-income homes ... $30 million Choice Neighborhoods Implementation grant ... over $60 million through D3.

rows.supporting_quote: The Mariposa District redevelopment ... expected to cost $197 million, cover 14 acres and add 581 multi-family, mixed-income units ... people living in the existing affordable housing were not displaced.

rows.supporting_quote: DHA is exploring a major redevelopment of the Westridge and Mulroy affordable housing communities near the Knox Light Rail Station ... 600 to 700 affordable housing units ... first phase focuses on 133 new housing units.
